Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: OOPA Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: On 2015-07-30, OnOnPay Vietnam Mobile Services JSC assigned its intellectual property, including the domain names "ononpay.com" and "ononpay.vn", to OOPA Pte Ltd under a Deed of Assignment.
On 2017-08-09, Bui Sy Phong and Ms Nguyen Thi Van assigned the shares in their names to OOPA by a Deed of Assignment. OOPA beneficially owned OnOnPay as to 60% directly and 40% indirectly.
OOPA Pte Ltd was set up in Singapore for the purpose of holding the Vietnamese operating company, OnOnPay Vietnam Mobile Services JSC.
Bui Sy Phong secured investors for the group, including Captii Ventures Pte Ltd as lead investor.
In late 2018, OnOnPay began to undertake a procurement and supply business for small retailers in Vietnam, described internally as the Central Supply Business.

On 2019-01-11, Captii sent an email to Bui Sy Phong referring to OOPA's ownership of intellectual property used in the Central Supply Business, including the merchant list and the domain name "ononpay.com".

On 2019-01-16, Bui Sy Phong sent an email to the directors and shareholders of OOPA stating that OnOnPay had launched a central supply platform branded as Telio and suggesting the establishment of a new entity whose capitalisation table would fully reflect OnOnPay's.
On 2019-01-21, a new Singapore holding company named Telio Pte Ltd was incorporated by Bui Sy Phong as its sole shareholder.
On 2019-01-28, Telio Vietnam Co Ltd was incorporated in Vietnam as a wholly owned subsidiary of Telio Pte Ltd.

On 2019-01-25, Ms Nguyen Thi Van emailed OOPA's company secretary, copying Bui Sy Phong, requesting preparation of board resolutions to approve the spin-off of the merchandising business and the transfer of resources from OOPA-OnOnPay into Telio.
On 2019-01-24, Low Zhen Hui from Captii emailed Bui Sy Phong outlining steps for a new holding structure where Bui would transfer 100% ownership of Telio to the OOPA Group.
On 2019-01-30, Bui Sy Phong emailed a transition plan stating that Telio and OOPA would be held by basically the same group of shareholders and that all OnOnPay resources would be moved to Telio.
On 2019-01-14, in a Facebook Messenger conversation with a potential investor, Bui Sy Phong stated the Central Supply Business would be separated from OnOnPay and placed under Telio.
On 2018-12-15, Bui Sy Phong posted on Facebook that the launch of Telio confirmed OOPA's vision to support mom-and-pop shops.
At the material time, the directors of OOPA included Bui Sy Phong and Ng Sai Kit.
Soon after incorporating Telio, Bui Sy Phong applied for Telio to enter the Surge Ventures accelerator program.
As at early 2019, Bui Sy Phong held 72.09% of OOPA shares, which was subject to a dilution structure reducing his stake to 40.15% upon implementation of certain agreements.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the Central Supply Business and the Telio corporate structure, developed using OOPA group resources and OnOnPay operations, should be treated as a corporate opportunity and asset of OOPA.
Party_Details.Rule: A company director is required to use company resources and business developments for the benefit of the company and not to appropriate for personal benefit a business opportunity developed using those resources.
Party_Details.Application: OOPA, through its subsidiary OnOnPay, developed the Central Supply Business using its own intellectual property and networks. Internal communications between OOPA directors and the company secretary in late 2018 and early 2019 contemplated that the business would be spun off into Telio with a shareholding structure mirroring OOPA's. During this transition, Bui Sy Phong incorporated Telio as its sole shareholder and used OnOnPay's performance metrics to secure external investment through Telio.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether OOPA was the proper plaintiff, the nature of the Central Supply Business (CSB) as a maturing business opportunity, and whether that opportunity belonged to OOPA. It applied the principle from Canadian Aero Services Ltd v O'Malley, as approved in Tokuhon, that a director is disqualified from usurping for personal benefit a maturing business opportunity which the company is actively pursuing. The judge found that the CSB had moved beyond a mere idea, based on contemporaneous communications where Bui described good traction, revenue growth and profitability, and where he reported to OOPA's board about the CSB and proposed a new entity whose cap table would reflect OOPA's. The court held that the CSB was being undertaken by OnOnPay using OOPA's intellectual property and resources, and that OOPA, as holding company, had the right to decide whether to keep the CSB within OnOnPay or spin it off into Telio. Emails from Van to the company secretary, copied to Bui, requesting OOPA board resolutions to approve the spin-off and transfer of resources, and Bui's own follow-up emails, were treated as evidence that Telio was being incorporated on OOPA's behalf. The judge rejected Bui's contention that the CSB was his personal idea and that only OnOnPay, not OOPA, could complain. The court concluded that OOPA was the proper plaintiff and that the same conduct could constitute breaches of duty to both parent and subsidiary without negating OOPA's rights. On breach, the judge found that Bui moved the CSB into Telio, denied OOPA's ownership of Telio, and caused Telio to enter into arrangements with Surge without informing OOPA, thereby usurping a maturing business opportunity pursued by OOPA. The court held that Bui owed fiduciary duties of loyalty and candour to OOPA and that he failed to discharge them. In assessing an express trust, the court examined the three certainties. It inferred certainty of intention from Bui's 16 January 2019 email proposing a new entity whose cap table would fully reflect OOPA's, and from subsequent correspondence showing that Bui was expected to transfer Telio shares in accordance with a structure to be agreed by OOPA. Certainty of subject matter was found because all Telio shares issued at incorporation were to be held on trust for OOPA until directions were given, and certainty of objects was satisfied because OOPA was identified as the beneficial owner. The judge therefore held that Bui received and held the Telio shares as OOPA's nominee on express trust. In the alternative, the court held that even if an express trust had not been established, an institutional constructive trust would arise over the Telio shares as the fruits of Bui's usurpation of OOPA's corporate opportunity, drawing an analogy with Guy Neale v Nine Squares Pty Ltd, where trade marks registered in a company name were held on trust for a partnership. On the Companies Act s 391(1) defence, the court considered whether Bui had acted honestly and reasonably. It found that Bui knew the CSB did not belong to him personally, relying on his Facebook post describing Telio as confirming OOPA's vision, his use of OnOnPay's CSB track record in investor pitches, and his participation in emails treating Telio as an OOPA group spin-off. The judge rejected Bui's explanations as implausible and held that he had not acted honestly or reasonably, so no relief under s 391(1) was available. The court also declined to grant an equitable allowance, citing Mona Computer Systems v Singaravelu Murugan and emphasising that such allowances should be sparingly granted and that Bui's lack of honesty and candour militated against any allowance. The judge noted that Bui would still benefit indirectly through his shareholding in OOPA and that any loans he made to OnOnPay remained debts. As to remedy, the court declared that all Telio shares registered in Bui's name, whether issued at incorporation or later, were held on trust for OOPA, either under an express trust or, for later-issued shares, under a constructive trust as fruits of the breach. The court ordered Bui to transfer his Telio shares to OOPA and to account for any dividends, with reimbursement to Bui for any incorporation expenses or capital contributions he had made in respect of those shares, but without interest and without allowance for non-financial contributions or personal loans for Telio's development. Costs were awarded to OOPA on a standard basis, including disbursements for its expert, and specific costs orders were made for interlocutory applications. The final outcome was that Bui was held liable to OOPA, and OOPA obtained declaratory and proprietary relief over the Telio shares.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Bui Sy Phong

Facts.Text: By 2018, Bui Sy Phong was a Vietnamese entrepreneur who had developed a start-up venture initially involving mobile top-up tools and subsequently an e-wallet application in Vietnam.
Bui Sy Phong secured investors for his venture, including Captii Ventures Pte Ltd and Gobi MAVCAP ASEAN Investment Management Limited.
Bui Sy Phong was a director of OOPA Pte Ltd and its subsidiary OnOnPay Vietnam Mobile Services JSC.
Bui Sy Phong held 72.09% of the shares in OOPA Pte Ltd, subject to a dilution structure reducing his stake to 40.15%.
As CEO of OnOnPay, Bui Sy Phong oversaw the commencement of the Central Supply Business, a procurement business for small retailers in Vietnam.
On 2018-12-15, Bui Sy Phong publicly associated the launch of Telio with OOPA's vision via social media.
On 2019-01-14, Bui Sy Phong indicated to potential investors that the Central Supply Business would be placed under a new entity named Telio.
On 2019-01-16, Bui Sy Phong suggested to OOPA's board the establishment of a new entity whose capitalisation table would mirror OOPA's existing structure.
On 2019-01-21, Bui Sy Phong incorporated Telio Pte Ltd in Singapore as its sole shareholder.
On 2019-01-28, Telio Vietnam Co Ltd was incorporated in Vietnam as a wholly owned subsidiary of Telio Pte Ltd.
Bui Sy Phong circulated corporate structure diagrams showing options for Telio's ownership, following a proposal for him to transfer ownership of Telio to the OOPA group.
On 2019-01-25, an email copied to Bui Sy Phong requested OOPA board resolutions to approve the spin-off of the business and the transfer of assets into Telio.
Bui Sy Phong emailed a transition plan stating that Telio and OOPA would be held by the same group of shareholders and that OnOnPay resources would be moved to Telio.
By early 2019, Bui Sy Phong was using the merchant base of the Central Supply Business operated through OnOnPay to solicit external investment for Telio.
On 2019-03-19, Bui Sy Phong signed an investment agreement with Surge Ventures on behalf of Telio.
Bui Sy Phong participated in an OOPA board meeting on 2019-04-15 to discuss the ownership of Telio.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the Central Supply Business and Telio structure developed using OnOnPay and OOPA resources belong to OOPA, and whether Bui's conduct in incorporating Telio as its sole shareholder constitutes a breach of fiduciary duty.
Party_Details.Rule: A director who develops a new business line using the resources and operational platform of an existing company must align the benefits of that business with the company and its stakeholders, avoiding the diversion of economic benefits without approval.
Party_Details.Application: Bui Sy Phong held controlling roles in OOPA and OnOnPay and used OOPA-owned assets to launch the Central Supply Business. He initially proposed a structure where Telio’s shareholding mirrored OOPA’s. However, he incorporated Telio in his own name and secured external investment for it while OOPA’s board was still considering the spin-off resolutions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered Bui's arguments that OOPA was not the proper plaintiff, that any misconduct related only to OnOnPay, that the Central Supply Business (CSB) was his personal idea, and that the CSB had not matured into a business opportunity belonging to OOPA. It rejected these arguments, holding that a director can owe fiduciary duties to both a parent and its subsidiary and that the same conduct can infringe duties to both. The judge found that the CSB was a maturing business opportunity actively pursued within the OOPA group, based on Bui's own contemporaneous communications describing traction, revenue and profitability, and his 16 January 2019 email proposing a new entity whose cap table would fully reflect OOPA's. The court held that OOPA, as holding company, had the right to decide whether to house the CSB within OnOnPay or in a new entity, and that Bui's incorporation of Telio and Telio Vietnam, and his use of OnOnPay's CSB track record to solicit investors, occurred in that context. The judge found that emails from Van to the company secretary, copied to Bui, requesting OOPA board resolutions to approve the spin-off and transfer of resources, and Bui's forwarding of Low's email and corporate diagrams to the company secretary, showed that Telio was being set up on OOPA's behalf. The court did not accept Bui's later claim that he disagreed with Van's email, noting that he raised no contemporaneous objection and that his subsequent emails were consistent with the spin-off plan. The judge concluded that Bui understood that the CSB and Telio structure were to be aligned with OOPA's shareholder base and that he was expected to transfer Telio shares in accordance with a structure to be agreed by OOPA. On the question of honesty and the Companies Act s 391(1) defence, the court found that Bui knew the CSB did not belong to him personally, relying on his Facebook post describing Telio as confirming OOPA's vision, his investor communications referencing OnOnPay's CSB performance, and his participation in internal emails treating Telio as an OOPA group spin-off. The judge rejected Bui's explanation that he misrepresented ownership to investors to justify giving OOPA investors free shares, finding this implausible and inconsistent with the contemporaneous record. The court held that Bui did not act honestly or reasonably when he proceeded to sign investment documents with Surge Ventures on behalf of Telio without informing OOPA or obtaining its consent, in contrast to his earlier practice of keeping OOPA informed about potential investors such as Nextrans. As a result, the court declined to grant relief under s 391(1). On remedies, the judge held that Bui's Telio shares were either held on express trust for OOPA, based on the three certainties, or, in the alternative, on constructive trust as the fruits of his usurpation of OOPA's corporate opportunity. The court ordered Bui to transfer his Telio shares to OOPA and to account for dividends, with limited reimbursement for any incorporation expenses or capital contributions but no equitable allowance for his efforts or loans in developing Telio, given the need to avoid encouraging fiduciaries to act in breach. The court therefore held Bui liable and granted proprietary relief in favour of OOPA.
